content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES

BODLE, FOGEL, JULBER, REINHARDT AND ROTHSCHILD
5900 WILSHIRE BOULEVARD, SUITE 2500, LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90036
TELEPHONE: (213) 937-5250                                CABLE ADDRESS: BOFOLLAW


March 17, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention:    Robert Stern

Dear Commissioners:

This will confirm my telephone conversation with Mr. Robert
Stern concerning the request set forth below for an opinion
at the earliest possible date.

I am a member of the Board of Police Commissioners of the
City of Los Angeles. The Board is appointed by the Mayor,
subject to confirmation of the City Council. We meet one
afternoon a week and receive the generous compensation of
$10.00 per meeting. I, as well as two of the other five
Commissioners, am an attorney. The Commission has author-
ized me, as an individual Commissioner, to request an
opinion from you concerning the issue posed below.

On last Thursday, the Board of Police Commissioners was pre-
sented with a proposed Conflict of Interest Code for adoption.
The Board deferred action, in part in order to obtain an
opinion from your Commission. The Los Angeles City Council,
which has final authority to approve or disapprove and thus
ultimately enact the Conflict of Interest Code applicable to
our Commission, has adopted a deadline which requires us to
submit our proposed Code no later than April 1. I understand
that the other Los Angeles City Commissions which are
similarly appointed and compensated are under the same
deadline.

The proposed Code furnished to our Commission contains a
provision which would require any attorney with a ten percent
or greater interest in a law firm to disclose the names of
all clients of the firm from whom compensation in excess of

Fair Political Practices Commission
March 17, 1976
page two

$1,000 is received. Although no formal vote of the Commission was taken, it was the sense of the Commission that such a provision was unacceptable for legal, ethical and policy reasons. The Code also contained another provision, the effect of which would be to require one of the Commissioners, who is an attorney, to disclose the names of all clients of another law firm in which that Commissioner's spouse is a partner. This too, of course, was deemed unacceptable.

The effect of the provisions, as proposed to the Commission, would be to permit attorneys in very large law firms where partners have less than a ten percent interest not to disclose the names of their clients, but to require partners in small or medium sized firms to do so. Moreover, the effect would be to require senior partners in many firms who wished to serve on commissions to disclose the firm's clients, but to allow middle level or junior partners in those same firms not to make such disclosures. The ultimate effect, in the view of the members of our Commission, would be to eliminate as members of citizen commissions senior partners in all but the largest firms and all partners in smaller firms, but to permit other attorneys to serve in that capacity.

The attorney members of our citizen commission unanimously agreed that an attorney who was required to disclose all of the clients of the firm in which he was a partner would be unable to serve on a commission. We understand this view is shared by attorney members of other citizen commissions in the City of Los Angeles. In this connection, we do not think it would be possible or practical, even if it were ethical, to seek and obtain the consent to disclosure of all clients of affected law firms. We might point out that a similar, and equally serious, problem exists with respect to certain other professions, including psychiatrists.

In light of the above, we hereby request your opinion as to whether the Political Reform Act of 1974 requires that a local body adopting a Conflict of Interest Code applicable to a citizen commission must include a provision requiring attorney members of such citizen commissions to disclose the names of all clients represented by their law firms if such attorneys have a ten percent or greater interest in their firm and the compensation received from each such client exceeds $1,000. In other words, would the failure to include the above disclosure provision constitute a violation of the Political Reform Act of 1974? In considering the

Fair Political Practices Commission
March 17, 1976
page three

above question, you may assume that the Code actually
adopted contains a provision requiring an attorney member of
a citizen commission to disclose the name of any client of
the law firm who is substantially or directly affected by
any matter before the commission or who appears before the
commission, if such commissioner participates in the pro-
ceedings. In the event that the type of provision referred
to in the preceding sentence is insufficient, we request
your opinion as to what type of provision would comply with
the mandatory requirements of the Political Reform Act and
at the same time ensure that disclosure of the attorney-
client relationship will be made only when such disclosure
is necessary and material to matters before the Commission.

Assuming that a Conflict of Interest Code which does not
require the disclosure of all clients of attorneys, psychia-
trists, doctors and similar professionals serving on citizen
commissions may lawfully be adopted so long as disclosure of
particular clients is required whenever such clients' in-
terests are affected by matters before the commission, we
further request your opinion as to whether the Code may
treat disclosure of the names of customers or clients of
persons engaged in other businesses (e.g. real estate brokers,
bankers, executives of department stores, automobile dealers,
etc.) in a similar manner.

We would greatly appreciate your furnishing us with at least
a preliminary or partial opinion concerning the above so
that we may take action, with your guidance, prior to the
last day on which the Commission can act under the Council
requirement - Thursday, March 25. If for any reason it is
impossible for you to give us an opinion by that time, we
still request that you advise us of your conclusions at the
earliest possible date, since I am informed by our City
Attorney that the Council may take final action any time
after April 1, but must in any event act no later than
ninety days from the date of receipt of the proposed Codes
from the various commissions. I trust apologies for the time
problems created by this request. I very much regret that
this matter was not presented to our Commission at an earlier
date.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Stephen Reinhardt

cc: Sam Williams, Esq.
Mariana R. Pfaelzer, Esq.
Tony Alperin, Deputy City Attorney
